statement.en: Radiative Decoupling as a Boundary Condition: A Geometric Normalization from Stellar Photospheres to the Cosmic Horizon
statement.summary: Macroscopic astrophysical parameters are operationally inferred at radiative decoupling surfaces. We define a dimensionless boundary invariant, , coupling measurable radiative observables with Newtonian surface gravity. Under standard macroscopic closures, reduces identically to a geometric capacity . Using strictly independent solar measurements yields . Evaluating for a benchmark sample of 190 detached eclipsing-binary components shows tight clustering at , with no systematic trend over . We then project this empirically established local decoupling normalization to the vacuum boundary of the cosmic horizon, obtaining the unified boundary relation . This yields as a parameter-free geometric fraction. In this boundary formulation, the canonical vacuum discrepancy is a dimensional mismatch

reiTyping.dfumt8Reason: The axiom presents a novel dimensional/geometric framework (radiative decoupling boundary invariant) derived from stellar observations and projected to cosmological scales. The core claims are: (1) empirical—stellar sample clustering is measurable; (2) theoretical—the projection to cosmic horizon is speculative and not yet independently validated; (3) the 'parameter-free' cosmological prediction requires external confirmation. Status: preprint (2026-04-25), not yet peer-validated. The framework is internally consistent but remains provisional pending independent replication and theoretical justification for horizon-scale extrapolation.
